title: Primitive vs reference types
question_text: Which of the following is a primitive type in most languages?
explanation_text: Integers are primitive value types in nearly all languages.

title: Type of a boolean
question_text: What value can a boolean variable hold?
explanation_text: Booleans are limited to true and false.

title: Loop choice
question_text: Which loop is best when you know the iteration count in advance?
explanation_text: for loops are designed for known iteration counts.

title: Switch fallthrough
question_text: What keyword prevents fallthrough in a typical switch statement?
explanation_text: break exits the switch and prevents the next case running.

title: Pure functions
question_text: A pure function must:
explanation_text: Pure functions have no side effects and same output for same input.

title: Recursion base case
question_text: Why do recursive functions need a base case?
explanation_text: Without it, recursion never stops and the stack overflows.

title: Big-O of binary search
question_text: What is the worst-case time complexity of binary search on a sorted array?
explanation_text: Binary search halves the input each step: O(log n).

title: Sorting stability
question_text: Which sort is NOT stable in its standard form?
explanation_text: Quicksort is not stable in its standard partition.

title: Hash table average lookup
question_text: What is the expected average-case lookup time in a hash table?
explanation_text: With a good hash, lookups are O(1) average.

title: Constants
question_text: Which keyword commonly declares a constant in modern languages?
explanation_text: const is the typical keyword in JS, TS, C++, etc.

title: Primary key
question_text: What does a primary key uniquely identify?
explanation_text: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a table.

title: Foreign key purpose
question_text: A foreign key enforces:
explanation_text: Foreign keys enforce referential integrity between tables.

title: SELECT clause role
question_text: Which SQL clause is used to choose specific columns?
explanation_text: SELECT chooses the columns to project.

title: JOIN type
question_text: Which JOIN returns only rows that match in both tables?
explanation_text: INNER JOIN returns matching rows from both sides.

title: Aggregation
question_text: Which clause is required when using non-aggregated columns alongside aggregates?
explanation_text: Non-aggregated SELECT columns must appear in GROUP BY.

title: 1NF
question_text: First normal form requires that:
explanation_text: 1NF requires atomic, indivisible column values.

title: 3NF goal
question_text: Third normal form removes:
explanation_text: 3NF removes transitive dependencies on non-key attributes.

title: Index trade-off
question_text: What is the main downside of adding an index to a table?
explanation_text: Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and use space.

title: B-tree typical use
question_text: B-tree indexes are most efficient for:
explanation_text: B-trees excel at range and equality lookups on ordered data.

title: ACID 'I'
question_text: What does the 'I' in ACID stand for?
explanation_text: Isolation: concurrent transactions appear sequential.

title: DELETE without WHERE
question_text: What happens with DELETE FROM users; (no WHERE)?
explanation_text: Without WHERE, DELETE removes every row in the table.

title: Supervised learning
question_text: Which statement best describes supervised learning?
explanation_text: Supervised learning uses labeled examples to learn a mapping from inputs to outputs.

title: Training vs testing data
question_text: What is the main purpose of a test set?
explanation_text: A test set estimates how well the model performs on unseen data.

title: BFS property
question_text: What does BFS usually guarantee in an unweighted graph?
explanation_text: BFS explores by depth level and finds a shortest path in terms of number of edges.

title: A* evaluation function
question_text: Which formula is commonly used by A* search?
explanation_text: A* commonly evaluates nodes using f(n)=g(n)+h(n).

title: Activation function role
question_text: What is the role of an activation function in a neural network?
explanation_text: Activation functions add non-linearity, allowing the network to learn complex relationships.

title: Overfitting meaning
question_text: What does overfitting mean?
explanation_text: Overfitting happens when a model fits training data too closely and generalizes poorly.

title: Biased data risk
question_text: Why can biased training data be harmful?
explanation_text: Biased data can lead to unfair or inaccurate predictions for some groups.

title: RIP transport protocol
question_text: Which transport protocol does RIP use?
explanation_text: RIP uses UDP for routing updates.

title: IPv4 fragmentation reassembly
question_text: Where is an IPv4 fragmented packet reassembled?
explanation_text: IP fragments are reassembled only at the final destination.

title: TTL field purpose
question_text: What happens when the TTL field of an IP packet reaches zero?
explanation_text: TTL prevents packets from looping forever in the network.

title: DHCP allocation steps
question_text: What is the correct basic DHCP address allocation sequence?
explanation_text: DHCP commonly follows the DORA sequence: Discover, Offer, Request, ACK.

title: NAT main purpose
question_text: What is the main purpose of NAT?
explanation_text: NAT maps private internal addresses to one or more public addresses.

title: IPv6 address length
question_text: How many bits are in an IPv6 address?
explanation_text: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits long.

title: IPv4 address length
question_text: How many bits are in an IPv4 source or destination address field?
explanation_text: IPv4 source and destination addresses are each 32 bits.

title: Packet loss in routers
question_text: Where can packet loss occur inside a router?
explanation_text: Queues may overflow at both input and output ports.

title: Forwarding definition
question_text: What does forwarding mean in a router?
explanation_text: Forwarding is the data-plane action of moving packets to the proper output interface.

title: Control plane role
question_text: What is the role of the control plane?
explanation_text: The control plane is responsible for routing decisions and path selection.

title: Data plane role
question_text: What is the role of the data plane?
explanation_text: The data plane forwards packets based on tables computed by routing logic.

title: BGP transport protocol
question_text: Which transport protocol does BGP use?
explanation_text: BGP runs over TCP.

title: BGP loop prevention
question_text: Which BGP attribute helps prevent routing loops?
explanation_text: BGP can detect loops using the AS_PATH attribute.

title: OSPF shortest path algorithm
question_text: Which algorithm does OSPF commonly use to calculate shortest paths?
explanation_text: OSPF is a link-state protocol and uses Dijkstra for shortest path calculation.

title: OSPF routing type
question_text: What type of routing protocol is OSPF?
explanation_text: OSPF is a link-state routing protocol.

title: RIP metric
question_text: Which metric does RIP use to choose routes?
explanation_text: RIP selects routes mainly by hop count.

title: RIP maximum hop count
question_text: What is the maximum usable hop count in RIP?
explanation_text: RIP treats 16 as unreachable, so the maximum usable hop count is 15.

title: RIP update interval
question_text: How often does RIP send routing updates by default?
explanation_text: RIP commonly sends full routing updates every 30 seconds.

title: ICMP layer
question_text: To which layer does ICMP belong?
explanation_text: ICMP is part of the network layer and is carried directly over IP.

title: CRC result
question_text: In CRC, what value is appended to the data?
explanation_text: CRC appends the remainder obtained from polynomial division.

title: Link layer RDT scope
question_text: At what scope is reliable data transfer implemented in the link layer?
explanation_text: Link-layer reliability, when used, is between adjacent nodes on a link.

title: Switch forwarding basis
question_text: What address does a switch mainly use to forward frames?
explanation_text: Switches operate at the link layer and forward using MAC addresses.
